vitals.location: Manali to Leh, Himachal Pradesh / Ladakh, India

course_description.overview: The Manali-Khardung La Cycling Championship is a 7-day, 510 km ultra-endurance cycling race traversing the world's highest long-distance cycling route. Riders ascend six high-altitude passes including Khardung La (5,359m/17,982 ft), the second highest motorable road globally, with cumulative elevation gain of 35,000 feet across alpine Himalayan terrain.

key_segments.name: Manali to Marhi
key_segments.description: Initial climb through coniferous forests toward Rohtang Pass (3,978m), establishing altitude acclimatization

key_segments.name: Marhi to Sissu via Rohtang La
key_segments.description: High-altitude pass crossing at 3,978m with sustained climbing through alpine terrain

key_segments.name: Sissu to Jispa to Zingzingbar
key_segments.description: Progressive altitude gain to 4,270m, entering high-altitude plateau zone

key_segments.name: Zingzingbar to Sarchu via Baralacha La
key_segments.description: Crossing Baralacha La (4,850m), one of the expedition's most challenging passes

key_segments.name: Sarchu to Whisky Nala via Nakee La
key_segments.description: Entry into Ladakh via Nakee La (4,769m), crossing famous Gata Loops with technical descents

key_segments.name: Whisky Nala to Debring via Lachung La
key_segments.description: Longest stage crossing Lachung La (5,059m), extreme altitude exposure and technical terrain

key_segments.name: Debring to Leh
key_segments.description: Gradual descent along Indus Valley to Leh town at 3,500m

key_segments.name: Leh to Khardung La and return
key_segments.description: Final challenge: steep 2,000m elevation gain to Khardung La (5,359m), world's second highest motorable pass, with return descent

course_description.difficulty_narrative: Extreme ultra-endurance event combining sustained high-altitude exposure (6 passes above 4,000m), significant daily distances (73 km average), cumulative 35,000 ft elevation gain, and technical descents. Acute Mountain Sickness (AMS) is common at extreme elevations. Requires exceptional fitness, altitude acclimatization, technical bike handling, and mental resilience across 7 consecutive racing days in remote Himalayan terrain.

logistics.getting_there: Fly to Delhi or Chandigarh, then drive/fly to Manali (Himachal Pradesh). Manali serves as the race start point with hotel check-in and briefing the day before racing begins.
logistics.accommodation: Mix of hotel stays in Manali and Leh, with camping/dome tents and Swiss tents at high-altitude base camps during the 7-day race. Post-race accommodation in Leh hotels.
logistics.registration_details: Online registration required through official MKCC website. Medical certificate mandatory. Early registration recommended due to limited field size (~100 riders). Registration typically opens 4-6 months before event.
logistics.bike_logistics: Participants bring their own mountain bikes or rent locally. Support vehicles accompany the race with spare parts, fuel, and mechanical assistance. Bikes must be in excellent condition for extreme terrain.
logistics.local_considerations: High-altitude environment requires acclimatization (2-3 days recommended before racing). Extreme weather conditions possible (snow, rain, wind). Limited mobile connectivity in remote sections. Travel insurance strongly recommended. Permits may be required for Ladakh region. Best season is July-August for weather stability.

final_verdict.one_liner: The world's highest long-distance cycling race—a transcendent Himalayan ultra-endurance test combining extreme altitude, technical terrain, and notable scenery for elite mountain cyclists seeking ultimate adventure.
final_verdict.who_is_it_for: Experienced ultra-endurance cyclists with exceptional fitness, high-altitude acclimatization experience, technical bike handling skills, and mental fortitude. Requires 18-60 age eligibility and medical clearance. Not suitable for casual cyclists or those with altitude sensitivity.
final_verdict.is_it_worth_it: Yes, for serious adventure cyclists seeking a bucket-list Himalayan experience and the prestige of completing the world's highest long-distance cycling race. The notable scenery, extreme altitude exposure, and technical challenge justify the effort despite high costs and logistical complexity. Less worthwhile for those prioritizing value or competitive racing depth.
pro_tips: Arrive 5-7 days early for altitude acclimatization in Manali or Leh before racing begins
Train extensively at elevation if possible; high-altitude fitness cannot be replicated at sea level
Invest in quality high-altitude gear (insulated layers, windproof jacket, thermal gloves) as weather is unpredictable above 4,000m
Practice bike handling on technical descents before the race; Gata Loops and Lachung La descents demand precision and confidence
